note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's break tags and sentence pauses. Never rewrite, summarize, or reorder. Block windows are MEASURED onsets (FFT cross-correlation of each sNN.mp3 against the joined 8 kHz mono master): the nominal Sigma(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula overshoots the probed master by 0.53s (> 0.3 tolerance) because ffprobe's mp3 durations overstate the decoded sample counts; the cross-correlation shows every block joined at an exact 0.400s gap after the prior block's decoded audio. Master ffprobe duration 210.207s (xcorr 8 kHz decode 210.16s).

phrases.text: Open the Reports module.
Every figure a client asks you for starts here.
A hundred and seventy-three reports, already sorted so you're not digging through a folder.
The left sidebar is your way in.
Start at the top of the sidebar.
These five shortcuts are pre-built slices of the same list.
The ones you starred, the ones running on a timer, the analytics set.
When a client wants what they always want, you skip the search and click the view.
Below that, nineteen categories.
Financial, transactions, employee labor, inventory, and the rest,
each with a live count beside it.
Some are broad, like transactions with twenty-one reports.
Some hold just one, like weather.
This is how you turn a wall of reports into the two or three
that actually answer the question in front of you.
On the right, the list itself.
A search box sits above it.
Type a few letters of a report's name and the list shrinks to match.
Faster than paging through four screens when you already know what you're after.
Top right, one button: Schedule Report.
That's how a report gets put on a timer and mailed out on its own.
We're only pointing at it today.
It sends, so we leave it alone.
Now the columns.
A star for favorites, the report's name, its category, its type, and when it last ran.
Five columns tell you what a report is and whether anyone has touched it lately.
Look at the Type column.
Standard, Scheduled, or Analytics.
A dash under Last Run means nobody has run it yet.
A date and time means someone did.
The daily financial, for instance, has a scheduled twin that last ran in late March.
And the starred rows are your favorites, one click away in that view up top.
Click a report to open it.
Take the daily financial.
The list steps aside and the report viewer takes over.
Same idea as opening a file: you picked it, now you're inside it.
Across the top, the filters.
A location selector, a date range with a from and a to, and a More Filters button for the rest.
This is where you tell the report which sites and which days before you run it.
Two buttons frame it.
Run Report renders it.
Close Report backs you out.
Notice Schedule Report isn't here.
Scheduling stays on the hub; in here you only run and read.
Click Run Report.
Summary tiles and a table build from the filters you set.
Running it only reads the numbers, so nothing is sent and nothing is saved.
In the drill, you'll hunt down the per-location and per-shift rows,
and the refund column, before you close it.
Check the stamp on the result.
It carries the date the report was generated,
and in this demo it flags the figures as sample data.
On a real client tenant that same line reads their live numbers instead.
Done reading? Close Report.
The viewer folds away and the full list is back, exactly as you left it.
You ran a report and touched nothing.
That's the whole loop.
Find a report by view or category, open it,
set the location and the dates, run it, read it, close it.
When a client swears a report looks wrong, this is the screen you'll share with them.
Now you know every control on it,
and the next lesson takes you into the audit trail behind those numbers.
